Round 2 reveals that the secret contains exactly 2 of the 5 guessed colors (red, blue, green, yellow, white), and none are in the correct position. The other 3 guessed colors are absent, but we don't know which. No other constraints yet.

Round 3 says exactly 3 of {green, yellow, white, black, purple} are in secret. This yields two possible cases: (a) exactly 1 of B is present, along with exactly 1 of {red, blue} and both black and purple; (b) exactly 2 of B are present, with 0 of {red, blue} and exactly 1 of {black, purple}. No colors are yet confirmed impossible because both cases are still viable. The locked position from round 3 belongs to one of the three present colors from that guess. Also, the two present colors from round 2 are misplaced relative to their round 2 positions. Overlapping colors from B (if present) are misplaced in round 2 and may be locked or misplaced in round 3. The secret has 5 positions, so duplicates exist in both cases.

Round 4 repeats the exact same guess and feedback as Round 2, confirming the same constraints. The combined knowledge from all rounds still leaves two possible cases: (Case A) secret contains exactly 1 of {green, yellow, white}, exactly 1 of {red, blue}, and both black and purple; or (Case B) secret contains exactly 2 of {green, yellow, white}, 0 of {red, blue}, and exactly 1 of {black, purple}. The locked position from Round 3 (1 correct position) further constrains which color is where, but we cannot yet pinpoint it. No colors are confirmed present or absent.

Round 5 repeats the same guess and feedback as rounds 2 and 4, confirming that exactly 2 of {red, blue, green, yellow, white} are in the secret, both misplaced. Combined with round 3's constraint that exactly 3 of {green, yellow, white, black, purple} are in the secret, and exactly one of those three is at a locked position, we now have three possible values for the number of common colors (c = secret ∩ {green, yellow, white}): c=0,1,2.
